title: Amate bark paper
subtitle: The pre-Columbian Mexican papermaking method: cook, beat, and dry
excerpt: Amate is bark paper made by cooking fig-tree or mulberry bark in an alkaline solution, then beating the softened strips flat on a board until the fibres mat together. No mould, no water suspension: the sheet forms by beating, not sieving.
sourceNotes: Pre-Columbian papermaking in Mesoamerica described in the US Bureau of American Ethnology Bulletin series (late 19th and early 20th century): federal publications in the public domain. The Otomi tradition of San Pablito, Puebla, Mexico is documented in anthropological reports from the same era. The alkaline cook method (lime or ash lye) follows the general principles of bast-fibre preparation common across multiple papermaking traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Bark paper made in Mesoamerica from the inner bast fibre of fig trees or paper mulberry. The pre-Columbian name for this paper in Nahuatl; still produced by Otomi communities in Puebla, Mexico.

The inner fibrous layer beneath a plant's bark. In papermaking and textile work, the bast is the source of long, strong cellulose fibres. Flax (linen), mulberry (washi), and fig (amate) are all bast-fibre materials.

An alkaline solution made by leaching water through wood ash. It contains potassium carbonate and potassium hydroxide. Used in traditional textile and fibre-processing to soften plant material by breaking down the lignin and pectin that bind the fibres.

content.text: In most papermaking traditions, the sheet forms in water: fibres are suspended in a vat, a mould is dipped in, and water drains through the mesh leaving a mat of fibres on the surface.

are softened in an alkaline cook, rinsed, then beaten flat on a board in an overlapping grid. The fibres mat together under the repeated blows. The sheet dries on the board. The result is a thick, textured paper with a visible grid pattern from the bark strips: not smooth like a mould-formed sheet, but strong and characteristic.

attrs.title: Working with ash lye and lime
attrs.body: Wood ash lye (potassium hydroxide solution) and calcium hydroxide (lime water) are alkalis that can irritate skin and eyes. Wear nitrile gloves and avoid splashing. Rinse any contact with skin promptly under running water. Work in a ventilated space. Do not use aluminium cookware with alkaline solutions: the alkali reacts with the metal.

items.name: Fig tree bark strips, stripped and soaked overnight
items.substitutions: Paper mulberry bark (kozo) is a reliable substitute with similar bast-fibre structure. Other bast-bearing tree barks (e.g. mulberry, breadfruit) also work in the traditional Mesoamerican method.

items.name: Wood ash lye or calcium hydroxide (lime) solution
items.qty: Enough to cover the bark in a pot
items.substitutions: Soda ash (sodium carbonate) solution at 2% concentration substitutes for both ash lye and lime, and is easier to source consistently.

items.name: Smooth stone for beating
items.substitutions: A smooth hardwood mallet, a smooth piece of dense plastic, or the flat back of a spoon all work. The beating surface should be smooth and dense enough to compress the fibres rather than tearing them.

items.name: Flat smooth board, slightly larger than the intended sheet
items.substitutions: A piece of smooth hardwood, a thick glass sheet, or a ceramic tile. The board acts as both the beating surface and the drying surface.

content.text: Soak the bark strips in plain water overnight. This begins softening the outer bark and makes the cook more effective.
Place the soaked bark in a pot and cover with the

or lime solution. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for one to two hours, until the bark is soft and the outer bark peels away from the inner bast fibre easily. The cooking time varies with the age and thickness of the bark.

Rinse the cooked bark thoroughly under running water. Rinsing is important: residual alkali in the fibre will continue to break down the cellulose over time and weaken the finished sheet. Rinse until the water runs clear and the bark no longer feels slippery.
Peel away any remaining outer bark from the cooked strips. The inner bast fibre should be pale, flexible, and slightly translucent. Lay it in a bowl of clean water while you prepare the board.

Place the board on a flat, stable surface. Lay strips of the damp bast fibre in a single layer on the board, orientated horizontally, edges just overlapping. The strips should be no wider than about 10-15 mm; wider strips do not mat together well.

Lay a second layer of strips vertically across the first, again with edges just overlapping. You now have a grid of bark strips. The intersecting perpendicular layers are what gives the finished amate its visible grid texture.

Begin beating the grid firmly and evenly with the smooth stone, working from the centre outward. The strips spread under the blows and the fibres from the horizontal and vertical layers begin to interlock at the cross-points. Keep the stone flat against the surface; do not rock the edge into the paper. Beat for several minutes, then check the sheet: it should be becoming cohesive and the grid pattern should be visible but smooth.

Leave the beaten sheet to dry on the board in a warm place or in gentle sunlight. Do not peel it from the board until fully dry. The amate shrinks slightly as it dries and will release from a smooth surface cleanly once dry. A low oven (50-60°C) with the door slightly ajar shortens the drying time.

items.cause: Bark was not cooked long enough, or the strips are too dry
items.fix: Cook for longer and keep the strips wet throughout the beating. If they start to dry out, splash on a little water before continuing to beat.

items.problem: Sheet does not hold together; falls apart when lifted
items.cause: Insufficient beating, or strips were too thick to mat properly
items.fix: Beat more thoroughly and for longer. Strips wider than 15 mm do not interlock well: re-prepare with narrower strips.

items.cause: Over-beaten, or alkali residue weakening the fibres
items.fix: Rinse the bark more thoroughly after cooking. Do not beat until the fibres are transparent or mushy: stop when the sheet is cohesive but still shows some texture.

content.text: Finished amate has a distinctive texture quite unlike mould-formed paper. It takes paint and ink well once sealed, and the visible grid pattern is part of the aesthetic rather than a defect. Traditional uses include painting surfaces for indigenous Otomi art, where the grid provides a visual rhythm beneath painted figures. At home it works as a journal cover material, a background for collage, or as a texture study alongside mould-formed sheets.
